meta.title: Rust
meta.description: Review checklist for Rust applications and libraries

items.text: Are there unnecessary .clone() calls that could be replaced with borrows?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Cloning creates a deep copy of data. If a function only needs to read data, passing a reference (&T) avoids the allocation.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unnecessary clones waste memory and CPU. They also signal that the author may not fully understand ownership, which can hide real design issues.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search the diff for .clone(). For each one, check whether the receiving function actually needs ownership or could accept a borrow instead.
baseContent.exampleComment: This .clone() on the config struct can be avoided. process_config only reads it, so passing &config works here.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Borrow by default. Clone only when you genuinely need an independent copy.

items.text: Are lifetime annotations used correctly and only where necessary?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Lifetime annotations tell the compiler how long references are valid. Rust elides them when the rules are unambiguous, so explicit annotations should only appear when needed.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Incorrect lifetimes cause compile errors or force the programmer into awkward workarounds. Unnecessary annotations add visual noise.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that explicit lifetime annotations are justified. If a function has one input reference and one output reference, elision handles it automatically.
baseContent.exampleComment: The lifetime 'a on this function is inferred by elision rules. You can remove it to simplify the signature.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Let the compiler elide lifetimes. Add annotations only when the compiler asks.

items.text: Does the code handle move semantics correctly, avoiding use-after-move?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When a value is moved in Rust, the original binding is no longer valid. The compiler catches this, but the design intent matters for readability.
baseContent.whyItMatters: While the compiler prevents use-after-move, poor ownership design leads to excessive cloning or convoluted code restructuring.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for values consumed by one call that are needed later. Check if the API should take &self or &mut self instead of self.
baseContent.exampleComment: Builder::build() takes self by value, which means the builder cannot be reused. Is that intentional? Consider taking &self if reuse is expected.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Design APIs around whether they consume, borrow, or mutably borrow their arguments.

items.text: Is interior mutability (Cell, RefCell, Mutex) used appropriately?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Interior mutability lets you mutate data behind a shared reference. Cell and RefCell are for single-threaded use; Mutex and RwLock for multi-threaded.
baseContent.whyItMatters: RefCell panics at runtime if borrow rules are violated. Using RefCell where Mutex is needed (or vice versa) leads to data races or unnecessary overhead.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that RefCell is not shared across threads. Verify that Mutex/RwLock are not held across .await points. Ensure Cell is used for Copy types only.
baseContent.exampleComment: This RefCell<Vec<Event>> is stored in an Arc, which means it can be shared across threads. Switch to Mutex<Vec<Event>> for thread safety.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Single thread: Cell/RefCell. Multi-thread: Mutex/RwLock. Never mix them up.

items.text: Are .unwrap() and .expect() avoided in production code paths?

baseContent.whatItMeans: .unwrap() and .expect() panic on None/Err, crashing the program. They are acceptable in tests and truly impossible-to-fail cases, but not in general application code.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A panic in production brings down the entire process (or the async task). Proper error propagation with ? allows callers to decide how to handle failures.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search the diff for .unwrap() and .expect(). For each, ask: can this actually fail in production? If yes, replace with ? or match.
baseContent.exampleComment: This .unwrap() on the database query will crash the server if the DB is unreachable. Use ? to propagate the error to the handler.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
let user = db.find_user(id).unwrap();
let name = user.name.unwrap();

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
let user = db.find_user(id)?;
let name = user.name.ok_or(AppError::MissingName)?;

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Reserve unwrap for impossible-to-fail cases. Use ? everywhere else.

items.text: Are custom error types well-defined with thiserror or similar?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Libraries should define domain-specific error enums. thiserror derives Display and Error implementations. anyhow is for applications that do not need structured errors.
baseContent.whyItMatters: String errors (Box<dyn Error>) lose type information, making it impossible for callers to match on specific failures. Good error types enable proper error handling upstream.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if the crate defines its own error enum. Verify that each variant carries enough context. Ensure library code does not use anyhow (that is for binaries).
baseContent.exampleComment: This function returns Box<dyn Error>, which means callers cannot match on specific failures. Consider defining a dedicated error enum.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Libraries: thiserror with specific variants. Binaries: anyhow for convenience.

items.text: Do errors carry sufficient context for debugging?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When propagating errors, adding context (e.g., the file path that failed to open) helps operators diagnose issues without needing a debugger.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A bare io::Error saying 'file not found' is useless without knowing which file. Context turns cryptic errors into actionable messages.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check error propagation with ?. Is context added via .context() (anyhow) or by mapping into a contextual variant?
baseContent.exampleComment: This file read error will not tell us which config path failed. Add context: .context(format!("reading config from {path}"))?
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always attach the 'what were we doing' context when propagating errors.

items.text: Are panic boundaries established for FFI and thread boundaries?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Panics unwinding across FFI boundaries is undefined behavior. Panics in spawned threads crash silently unless caught with catch_unwind or JoinHandle.
baseContent.whyItMatters: An uncaught panic in a thread pool worker can silently drop work. A panic across FFI corrupts memory. Both are hard to debug in production.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check thread::spawn and tokio::spawn for panic handling. Check FFI exports for catch_unwind. Verify that panic=abort is not set when catch_unwind is used.
baseContent.exampleComment: This FFI-exported function does not use catch_unwind. If the Rust code panics, it is undefined behavior for the C caller.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Catch panics at thread and FFI boundaries. Never let them unwind into foreign code.

items.text: Are Send and Sync bounds correct for types shared across threads?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Send means a type can be transferred to another thread. Sync means it can be shared via &T across threads. The compiler enforces these, but unsafe code can opt out incorrectly.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Incorrect unsafe impl Send/Sync is a data race waiting to happen. It undermines Rust's core safety guarantee.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for unsafe impl Send and unsafe impl Sync. Verify the type truly is safe to send/share. Check that it does not contain raw pointers to thread-local data.
baseContent.exampleComment: This unsafe impl Send for Handle is risky since Handle wraps a *mut that points into thread-local storage. This can cause data races.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never add unsafe impl Send/Sync without proving the type is genuinely thread-safe.

items.text: Are Mutex guards not held across .await points?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Holding a std::sync::Mutex guard across an .await means the lock is held while the task is suspended, potentially blocking the executor thread.
baseContent.whyItMatters: This can deadlock the async runtime. Even with tokio::sync::Mutex (which is await-aware), holding locks across awaits increases contention.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for MutexGuard bindings that span an .await. The guard must be dropped before awaiting.
baseContent.exampleComment: The lock guard `data` is held across the .await on line 15. Drop it before awaiting, or restructure to copy the data out first.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Drop mutex guards before .await. Copy out what you need, release the lock, then await.

items.text: Is Arc used correctly for shared ownership across threads?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Arc provides thread-safe reference counting. It should wrap shared data, not individual fields. Arc<Mutex<T>> is the standard pattern for shared mutable state.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Wrapping each field in its own Arc creates fragmented ownership and makes it easy to get into inconsistent states. A single Arc<SharedState> is simpler.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that Arc is not cloned excessively or wrapped around types that are already cheap to clone (like small integers).
baseContent.exampleComment: Each field is wrapped in its own Arc<Mutex<_>>. Consider grouping related fields into a single struct behind one Arc<Mutex<State>>.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: One Arc<Mutex<SharedState>> beats five Arc<Mutex<field>> every time.

items.text: Is blocking I/O properly handled with spawn_blocking in async contexts?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Calling blocking operations (file I/O, DNS, CPU-heavy work) on an async runtime thread starves other tasks. Use tokio::task::spawn_blocking or tokio::fs for these.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A single blocking call on the tokio runtime thread can freeze all other tasks on that thread. This causes latency spikes and can cascade into timeouts.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for std::fs, std::net, or heavy computation inside async functions. These should use spawn_blocking or async equivalents.
baseContent.exampleComment: std::fs::read_to_string blocks the runtime thread. Use tokio::fs::read_to_string or wrap it in spawn_blocking.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never block the async runtime. Use spawn_blocking for anything that takes more than a few microseconds.

items.text: Is unsafe code minimized and well-documented?

baseContent.whatItMeans: unsafe blocks should be as small as possible, with a SAFETY comment explaining why the invariants are upheld.
baseContent.whyItMatters: unsafe disables the borrow checker. Without documentation, future maintainers cannot verify the invariants still hold after changes.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for unsafe blocks. Check that each has a // SAFETY comment. Verify that the unsafe block does not encompass more code than necessary.
baseContent.exampleComment: This unsafe block covers 20 lines but only one line actually needs unsafe. Narrow the scope and add a SAFETY comment.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Minimize unsafe scope. Document invariants with SAFETY comments.

items.text: Are raw pointer dereferences guarded by validity checks?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Dereferencing a raw pointer (*const T / *mut T) is undefined behavior if the pointer is null, dangling, or misaligned.
baseContent.whyItMatters: UB can cause anything from silent data corruption to security exploits. Rust's safety guarantees do not apply inside unsafe blocks.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that raw pointers are validated (non-null, properly aligned, within allocation bounds) before dereferencing.
baseContent.exampleComment: This raw pointer from FFI is dereferenced without a null check. Add a null check or use NonNull to encode the invariant.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Validate every raw pointer before dereferencing. Use NonNull where nullability is impossible.

items.text: Does the code avoid common sources of undefined behavior?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Common UB sources: creating invalid references, data races, calling functions with wrong ABIs, reading uninitialized memory, and breaking aliasing rules.
baseContent.whyItMatters: UB makes the program's behavior unpredictable. The compiler assumes UB never happens and optimizes accordingly, which can remove safety checks.
baseContent.howToVerify: Run Miri (cargo +nightly miri test) on unsafe code. Review transmute calls, union field access, and extern fn signatures.
baseContent.exampleComment: This transmute from u8 to bool is UB for any value other than 0 or 1. Use match or != 0 instead.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: When in doubt, run Miri. It catches many forms of UB at runtime.

items.text: Are complex constructors using the builder pattern?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Functions with many parameters should use the builder or options-struct pattern for clarity and extensibility.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Positional parameters beyond 3-4 are error-prone. Builders make call sites self-documenting and allow adding optional parameters without breaking callers.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for functions or constructors with 4+ parameters. Consider whether a builder or config struct would improve the API.
baseContent.exampleComment: ServerConfig::new has 7 parameters. A builder pattern would make call sites more readable and allow optional parameters with defaults.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code: let server = Server::new("0.0.0.0", 8080, 4, true, None, 30, false);

baseContent.keyTakeaway: When constructors grow beyond 3 parameters, switch to the builder pattern.

items.text: Are newtypes used to prevent type confusion?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Wrapping primitives in newtypes (struct UserId(u64)) prevents accidentally passing a user ID where a post ID is expected.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Two u64 parameters next to each other are easy to swap. Newtypes make the compiler catch these bugs at zero runtime cost.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for functions with multiple same-typed parameters (e.g., fn transfer(from: u64, to: u64, amount: u64)). Could newtypes prevent mistakes?
baseContent.exampleComment: transfer(from_id, to_id, amount) takes three u64s. Wrapping IDs in newtypes (UserId, AccountId) would prevent accidental swaps.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Newtypes are free at runtime and prevent an entire class of bugs.

items.text: Are trait bounds minimal and appropriate?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Generic functions should require only the traits they actually use. Overly broad bounds (T: Clone + Debug + Send + Sync + 'static) restrict callers unnecessarily.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Broad bounds reduce the set of types that can use the API. They also create confusing error messages when bounds are not met.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check generic bounds. Remove any trait that the function body does not actually use. Prefer where clauses for readability when bounds are complex.
baseContent.exampleComment: This function requires T: Clone but never clones the value. Remove the Clone bound to widen the set of usable types.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Only require the traits you use. Every unnecessary bound is a door closed to some caller.

items.text: Are pub items minimized and intentional?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Only items that form the public API should be pub. Internal helpers should use pub(crate) or remain private.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Every pub item is a commitment. Changing it later is a breaking change. Exposing internals encourages coupling.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new pub items. Are they meant to be used by external crates? Would pub(crate) suffice?
baseContent.exampleComment: This helper function is pub but only used within the crate. Change to pub(crate) to keep the public API surface small.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Default to private. Promote to pub(crate), then pub, only when needed.

items.text: Are heap allocations minimized in hot paths?

baseContent.whatItMeans: In performance-critical code, prefer stack allocation (arrays, SmallVec) over heap allocation (Vec, Box). Reuse buffers instead of reallocating.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Heap allocations involve the global allocator, which can be a bottleneck under contention. They also cause cache misses.
baseContent.howToVerify: Profile with cargo flamegraph or perf. Look for Vec::new() or String::new() in tight loops that could use pre-allocated buffers.
baseContent.exampleComment: This Vec is allocated inside the loop on every iteration. Move it outside the loop and call .clear() to reuse the buffer.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Allocate once, reuse often. Move allocations out of loops.

items.text: Are iterator chains preferred over manual index loops?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Rust iterators are zero-cost abstractions. Chains like .filter().map().collect() often optimize better than manual indexing because bounds checks are elided.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Iterator chains are more readable and less error-prone than manual indexing. The compiler can also vectorize them more easily.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for for i in 0..vec.len() { vec[i] } patterns. These can usually be replaced with .iter().enumerate() or just .iter().
baseContent.exampleComment: This manual index loop can be simplified to items.iter().filter(|i| i.active).map(|i| i.name).collect().
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Prefer iterator chains. They are more idiomatic and often faster.

items.text: Is Cow used where data may or may not need cloning?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Cow<str> (Clone on Write) avoids cloning when the borrowed form suffices. It only allocates when mutation is needed.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Functions that sometimes return borrowed data and sometimes owned data benefit from Cow, avoiding unnecessary allocations in the common case.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for functions that clone a string to return it, when the original could be returned as-is most of the time.
baseContent.exampleComment: normalize_path clones the input in every case, but most paths are already normalized. Cow<str> would avoid the clone in the common case.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Cow avoids cloning when you might not need to. Use it for functions that usually return the input unchanged.

items.text: Are unit tests in a #[cfg(test)] mod tests block?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Rust convention places unit tests in a mod tests block at the bottom of the source file, gated by #[cfg(test)] so they are excluded from release builds.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Following the convention makes tests discoverable. #[cfg(test)] ensures test helpers and test dependencies are not compiled into production.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that new tests are inside a #[cfg(test)] module. Integration tests go in the tests/ directory.
baseContent.exampleComment: These tests are at the module level without #[cfg(test)]. Move them into a mod tests block.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Unit tests in #[cfg(test)] mod tests. Integration tests in tests/.

items.text: Would property-based testing (proptest) catch edge cases here?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Property-based testing generates random inputs and verifies invariants hold. It excels at finding edge cases humans miss, like empty inputs, unicode, or integer overflow.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Hand-written test cases only cover what the author thinks of. Proptest explores the input space automatically.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if the code under test handles arbitrary inputs (parsers, serializers, algorithms). These are great candidates for proptest.
baseContent.exampleComment: This parser has 5 hand-crafted test cases. Consider adding a proptest that generates random valid inputs and verifies roundtrip parsing.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: If it parses, serializes, or transforms data, proptest it.

items.text: Are error paths and edge cases tested, not just the happy path?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Tests should cover error conditions, boundary values, empty inputs, and concurrent scenarios, not just the expected success case.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Happy-path tests give false confidence. Bugs live in error handling, edge cases, and unexpected input combinations.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if new error variants or edge cases have corresponding tests. Look for #[should_panic] or assert!(result.is_err()) tests.
baseContent.exampleComment: This function returns Err on invalid input, but there are no tests for the error case. Add a test that verifies the error variant and message.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Test failures as thoroughly as successes. That is where the bugs hide.

items.text: Are dependency features scoped to only what is needed?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Cargo features are additive. Enabling unused features pulls in extra code, increasing compile times and binary size.
baseContent.whyItMatters: default-features = true often enables things you do not need (like tokio's full feature set when you only need rt and macros).
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new Cargo.toml dependencies. Are default features disabled when only specific features are needed?
baseContent.exampleComment: tokio is added with default features. Consider default-features = false with only ["rt-multi-thread", "macros"] to reduce compile time.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Disable default features and enable only what you use.

items.text: Are dependencies version-pinned appropriately?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Cargo.lock pins exact versions for binaries. Libraries should use semver ranges in Cargo.toml. Check that Cargo.lock is committed for binaries but not for libraries.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without Cargo.lock, binary builds are not reproducible. Libraries should not pin to allow consumers flexibility.
baseContent.howToVerify: For binaries: Cargo.lock should be committed. For libraries: Cargo.lock should be in .gitignore. Check that version ranges are not overly broad (e.g., * instead of ^1.2).
baseContent.exampleComment: This binary crate does not commit Cargo.lock. Add it to ensure reproducible builds.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Commit Cargo.lock for binaries. Use semver ranges for libraries.

items.text: Have new dependencies been audited for security and maintenance?

baseContent.whatItMeans: New crate dependencies should be checked for known vulnerabilities (cargo audit), maintenance status, and transitive dependency count.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Each dependency is code you trust with your program's safety. Unmaintained or vulnerable crates are a supply chain risk.
baseContent.howToVerify: Run cargo audit. Check crates.io for recent releases, download counts, and maintenance status. Review the transitive dependency tree with cargo tree.
baseContent.exampleComment: This new dependency has not been updated in 2 years and has an open advisory. Consider an alternative or fork.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: cargo audit before merging. Check maintenance status of new dependencies.

items.text: Does the code pass clippy with no warnings?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Clippy catches common mistakes, non-idiomatic patterns, and performance pitfalls. CI should run cargo clippy -- -D warnings.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Clippy lints encode collective Rust community knowledge. Ignoring them often means missing a simpler or safer approach.
baseContent.howToVerify: Run cargo clippy -- -D warnings locally. Check if CI enforces clippy.
baseContent.exampleComment: Clippy warns about this manual match on Option. Use .map_or() or .unwrap_or_default() instead.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Treat clippy warnings as errors in CI. Fix them, do not suppress them without good reason.

items.text: Is pattern matching used idiomatically instead of if-let chains?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Rust's match expression is exhaustive and catches missing cases at compile time. Prefer match over chains of if let when handling enums.
baseContent.whyItMatters: match forces you to handle all variants. If a new variant is added, the compiler flags every match that misses it. if-let chains silently ignore new variants.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for if let Some(x) = ... else if let Some(y) = ... chains on enums. A match would be clearer and safer.
baseContent.exampleComment: This chain of if-let on the Result variants would be clearer as a match statement. It also ensures new variants are handled.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use match for enums. The exhaustiveness check is one of Rust's superpowers.

items.text: Are standard traits (Debug, Clone, PartialEq) derived where appropriate?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Most structs should derive Debug for logging, Clone if they are value types, and PartialEq for testing. Deriving is zero-effort and enables ecosystem interoperability.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without Debug, values cannot be printed in error messages or logs. Without PartialEq, values cannot be compared in assertions.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new struct definitions. Do they derive at least Debug? Would Clone or PartialEq be useful for tests?
baseContent.exampleComment: This struct does not derive Debug, which makes it impossible to log or include in error messages. Add #[derive(Debug)].
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Derive Debug on everything. Add Clone and PartialEq when they make sense.
